lead: A moving day in Aldeburgh carries its own particular demands, set by the town's long seafront, its narrow streets running back from the shingle and the visitors and festival crowds that fill the place in season. After years of carrying homes to this part of the Suffolk Heritage Coast from our Westerfield base, we know where the day can snag and how to keep it flowing. This checklist walks you through the run-up, the morning itself and your first hours in the new home, with the local detail that keeps an Aldeburgh move calm and well-managed.

sections.h: Two Weeks Out: Sort Access and Timing
sections.body:
In Aldeburgh, the first thing to settle is not packing but access, and timing carries real weight here. The older streets running back from the seafront are narrow, parking near the front and the High Street can be at a premium, and in the busier months and during the festival the town fills with visitors that complicate a removal vehicle's day. Work out early where a lorry can realistically stop, and tell us as soon as you can. With that knowledge we can plan properly, perhaps shuttling your belongings the final stretch with a smaller van, or timing the unload to avoid the worst of the seasonal crowds.

If your new home is towards the edge of the town or the wider IP15 area, the day is often simpler, with more room to manoeuvre and, in places, driveways and modern access, though it is still worth checking for any temporary restrictions. Either way, this fortnight is for confirming your moving date with us, running down the freezer, notifying the important people of your change of address and arranging mail redirection. If your chain is looking uncertain, talk to us now about storage rather than leaving it to the last moment.

Gather your packing materials this week too, or ask us to supply them. Good strong boxes, plenty of tape, bubble wrap for the fragile things and marker pens for labelling all make a real difference, and running out partway through is a guaranteed source of stress. Having everything you need in the house before you begin means the packing flows steadily rather than stalling each time you reach for materials that simply are not there when you need them.

With a week to go, packing should be well under way. Work methodically through the house one room at a time rather than darting about, and begin with the spaces you use least: the spare room, the loft, the garage and the shed. The loft is the classic trap, holding far more than anyone remembers, and finding heavy boxes up a ladder on moving morning throws the whole timetable out, which matters all the more when you have a tight unloading window arranged on a narrow Aldeburgh street.

Label every box clearly on the side as well as the top, marking which room it belongs to in your new Aldeburgh home along with a note of the contents. As we unload, we carry boxes straight to the right room, so good labels stop your belongings piling up in the hall. Colour coding works beautifully: a colour for each room, matching stickers on the boxes and a matching sheet on each door at the new house, so even a crew seeing the property for the first time can unload at speed, which is just what you want when seafront parking is limited.

Set aside anything precious, important documents, jewellery and small valuables, to travel in your own car rather than the lorry. Mark fragile boxes on more than one side, and keep a simple numbered inventory if any part of your move involves storage or a staggered handover. By the end of this week, the only things left to pack should be the kettle, the breakfast things and the beds you are sleeping in the night before the move itself.

The day before an Aldeburgh move is for finishing everything off. Defrost and empty the fridge and freezer, complete the last of the packing, and strip all but tonight's beds, so that when we arrive in the morning everything is sealed and ready except the kettle and the breakfast things. The single biggest cause of a slow, stressful moving day is unfinished packing when the crew turns up, and in Aldeburgh that delay can eat straight into a carefully arranged parking or access window, so being genuinely ready matters here more than in most places.

Pack your essentials box, the most important box of all, and keep it for your own car rather than the lorry. Into it goes the kettle, mugs, tea, milk and biscuits, phone chargers, basic toiletries, a change of clothes, any medication, important documents and a few toys for the children. This is the box that keeps everyone going, letting you make a brew the moment you arrive without opening the whole van. Take and note your meter readings at the old property, and charge your phone overnight ready for the day ahead.

Give a thought to the morning route as well. Aldeburgh gets busy, especially in season and during the festival, and the streets near the front can be congested with visitors. If your move involves driving through or near the centre, a little timing thought tonight saves frustration tomorrow, and an early start often pays dividends. Clear a path from the front door to the kerb at both properties, and if you have children or pets, arrange care for the day or set up one cleared room as their safe base.

sections.h: Moving Morning: Working with the Window
sections.body:
On the morning itself, being ready and being present is everything, and never more so than in a coastal town where the day can fill up with visitors. Open the curtains, switch on the lights so we can see clearly, and make sure nothing is still hidden in the loft, shed or behind a door. When we arrive we like a quick walk-round so you can point out the fragile boxes, the antiques and anything needing extra care, particularly if you are moving into or out of an older Aldeburgh home with narrow stairs and low doorways.

Leave out a kettle, mugs and a few biscuits for the team if you can; it keeps everyone going and it is the small kindness our crews always appreciate. Keep children and pets in their safe room with a clear note on the door, because the front door will stand open for much of the morning and cats in particular will bolt given half a chance, all the more worrying near the open seafront. Tell us where they are so we all keep that door shut. If parking is restricted, let us get straight to loading so we make full use of the window before the streets get busy.

Before we leave the old property, do a proper walk-through together. Check every cupboard, the loft, the shed, the garden and behind every door, because things always get left in the rush. Confirm the inventory, sort the handover of the old keys and make sure you have the keys for your new Aldeburgh home. Then meet us at the other end, where we will unload to the right rooms and rebuild the beds so you have somewhere comfortable to sleep that first night by the sea.

sections.h: Arriving in Aldeburgh: The First Hours
sections.body:
Arriving at the new home is where all that careful labelling pays off. As we unload, we carry each box to the room marked on its side, so even with a tight unloading window your home quickly takes shape rather than becoming one great heap in the hall. Point us to where the big furniture should go before we bring it in; placing a wardrobe correctly first time is far easier than shuffling it once it is full, and in older Aldeburgh properties with awkward corners that planning really counts. If you have colour-coded the doors, the whole unload moves faster and with fewer questions.

Get the kitchen working enough for a brew and a simple meal, and ask us to make up the beds before we leave. After a long day, the difference between a made-up bed and a mattress buried under boxes is the difference between a good first night in Aldeburgh and a miserable one. Find your essentials box, put the kettle on, and resist the temptation to unpack everything tonight. The boxes will still be there in the morning, and a proper rest is the best possible start in your new coastal home.

Over the following days, unpack at a sensible pace, room by room, and take time to enjoy where you now live. A first walk along the shingle, a stroll to the Scallop, fish bought fresh from the huts on the beach and a browse along the High Street are how a new house starts to feel like home. Register with a local GP and dentist, sort your utility and council accounts, and let yourself settle in gently. We will have done our part by then, and every load we carry is fully covered, so you can put your energy into enjoying life in Aldeburgh.

faqs: What should I plan first for a moving day in Aldeburgh?

Access and timing come first. The older streets running back from the seafront are narrow with limited parking, and the town fills with visitors in season and during the festival. Work out where a lorry can stop, tell us early, and we can use a shuttle van or time the unload to avoid the busiest periods.
Does the Aldeburgh Festival affect moving day?

It can. During the festival and the busy summer months, Aldeburgh fills with visitors, making parking and access near the front and centre harder. We plan moving days around the seasonal and festival bustle, often with an early start. Out of season, an Aldeburgh move generally runs more easily.
What should go in my essentials box for an Aldeburgh move?

Pack the kettle, mugs, tea, milk and biscuits, phone chargers, basic toiletries, a change of clothes, any medication, important documents and a few toys for children. Keep it in your own car so it is to hand the moment you reach your new Aldeburgh home and can make a brew straight away.
Can you move furniture into Aldeburgh's older seafront homes?

Yes. We regularly carry large and period pieces into older Aldeburgh homes with narrow stairs, tight hallways and low doorways near the front. We plan those challenges in advance, use a shuttle van where access is restricted and take real care. Every load we move is fully covered for complete peace of mind.
